text.title: Error Prevention — Good and Bad Examples
text.summary: Real product examples of effective error prevention and concrete anti-patterns to avoid.

example.note: Gmail compose: detects "attached" in message body without an attachment and prompts "Did you mean to attach a file?" — catches the slip before send. Also warns about empty subject line. Offers Undo Send for 30 seconds. Three prevention strategies in one flow.
Google Forms: all required fields marked, field types constrain input (date picker, dropdown), validation runs before submission with inline errors. Users cannot submit invalid data.

Amazon checkout: address validation with suggestions catches typos in real time. Payment validation before order is placed. Shipping timeline shown clearly so users confirm they are ordering the right option.

antipattern.description: Validation runs only on form submit, showing all errors at once at the top of the page after the user has completed the entire form.
antipattern.reason: Users must re-find each field and re-enter data, often losing unsaved work in other fields. Inline validation on blur prevents this entirely by catching errors one at a time as they occur.

antipattern.description: Accepting an invalid email address at entry and failing later when the email confirmation message cannot be delivered.
antipattern.reason: The error surface is now disconnected from the input — users have no way to diagnose or fix the problem without returning to the form. Inline email validation (format check on blur) catches this at the source.

antipattern.description: Using placeholder text as the only format hint, which disappears when the user starts typing.
antipattern.reason: Users who type quickly and then look up cannot see the format they were supposed to follow. Format hints must persist below or beside the field, not inside it.

antipattern.description: Applying a confirmation dialog to every navigation-away or form-close action, including cases where no data would be lost.
antipattern.reason: Unnecessary confirmations train users to click through without reading. When a genuinely important confirmation appears, it is dismissed with the same reflex — eliminating its protective value.

rule.statement: Track form abandonment rate, validation error frequency, submission error frequency, and support tickets mentioning specific fields as prevention effectiveness metrics.
rule.rationale: Without measurement, prevention improvements cannot be validated. A drop in validation errors after adding inline feedback is direct evidence of effectiveness.
